items.q: Why don't carriers of cystic fibrosis have symptoms?
items.correct: Carriers are heterozygous; their one normal CFTR allele makes enough working protein, so the recessive faulty allele is masked.
items.misconception: Carriers have a mild form of cystic fibrosis.
items.random_wrong: Carriers cannot pass the faulty allele to their children.
items.why: Students think a 'carrier' must show some disease rather than being a symptom-free heterozygote.

items.q: What does the faulty CFTR protein fail to do?
items.correct: It cannot transport chloride ions across membranes, so water movement changes and mucus becomes thick and sticky.
items.misconception: The faulty protein blocks oxygen from entering the lungs directly.
items.random_wrong: The faulty protein causes the lungs to fill with extra blood.
items.why: Students link the lung symptoms to gas exchange rather than to faulty ion transport.

items.q: Two carriers have a child. What is the chance it is affected?
items.correct: Each child has a 1 in 4 (25%) chance of being affected, independent of any previous children.
items.misconception: If the first child is affected, the next three will be healthy.
items.random_wrong: All children of two carriers will themselves be affected.
items.why: Students treat the 1-in-4 ratio as a fixed outcome over four children rather than a probability each time.

items.q: Cystic fibrosis is inherited as a:
items.why: Correct answer: recessive allele. “dominant allele” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: The faulty CFTR protein causes symptoms because it cannot transport:
items.why: Correct answer: chloride ions across membranes. “oxygen into the blood” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Two carrier parents (Ff x Ff) have a child. The probability the child is affected is:
items.why: The answer examiners want is: 1 in 4. “1 in 2” is the distractor this question was built around — if it tempted you, that's the exact gap to close.

items.q: Gene therapy aims to treat cystic fibrosis by:
items.correct: delivering a normal copy of the CFTR gene into cells
items.misconception: removing all chloride ions from the lungs
items.random_wrong: killing the bacteria that cause the mucus
items.why: It's delivering a normal copy of the CFTR gene into cells. “removing all chloride ions from the lungs” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Two cystic fibrosis carriers (Ff x Ff) have children. What percentage are expected to be affected (ff)?
items.why: Exact answer: 25 %. Estimation gets you near it; only the anchored number survives the exam hall.
